docs.title: Gaming Rebuilds
docs.text: A meta-repository for game rebuilds using agentic frameworks.

docs.title: What Is This?
docs.text:
This project explores rebuilding classic games using modern AI agent architectures. Each rebuild is an independent repository linked here as a git submodule.

The shared Agent Knowledge Base captures best practices and design patterns used across all rebuilds.

docs.title: Rebuilds
docs.text: Game Description Status Karriers Carrier-based strategy game rebuild In Progress

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• Experiment with multi-agent architectures applied to game logic
  • Build a reusable library of agent patterns for turn-based and strategy games
  • Document lessons learned for the broader agentic AI community

docs.title: AGENTS.md — Knowledge Base for Agentic Systems
docs.text: This document captures best practices, patterns, and guidelines for building agents in this project. It serves as the shared knowledge base across all rebuilds.

docs.title: 1. What Is an Agent?
docs.text:
An agent is an autonomous unit that perceives its environment, reasons over it, and takes actions to achieve a goal. In the context of this project, agents are built using agentic frameworks (e.g., Claude Agent SDK, LangGraph, CrewAI) and operate over game-domain tasks.

Key properties of a well-designed agent: - Goal-directed: every action traces back to a defined objective - Observable: the agent logs its reasoning and decisions - Bounded: the agent knows what it should and should not do - Composable: agents can be combined into multi-agent pipelines

docs.title: 2.1 Keep Tools Focused
docs.text:
Each tool the agent uses should do exactly one thing. Avoid multipurpose tools that mix concerns — they are harder to test, debug, and reason about.

# Good: single-responsibility tool
def get_player_position(player_id: str) -> dict:
    ...

# Avoid: tool doing too much
def manage_player(player_id: str, action: str, value: Any) -> dict:
    ...

docs.title: 2.2 Prefer Declarative Goals Over Procedural Scripts
docs.text: Give agents goals, not step-by-step procedures. Agents reason best when they understand the desired outcome rather than a fixed sequence of steps.

docs.title: 2.3 Trust but Verify Tool Outputs
docs.text: Never assume tool calls succeed. Always handle errors explicitly and design agents to retry or escalate when they encounter unexpected results.

docs.title: 2.4 Minimal Authority Principle
docs.text: Grant each agent only the tools and permissions it needs for its specific task. An agent responsible for reading game state should not have write access.

docs.title: 3.1 Orchestrator / Subagent
docs.text:
An orchestrator agent breaks down a high-level task and delegates to specialized subagents. Subagents report results back; the orchestrator synthesizes them.

Orchestrator
├── ResearchAgent   (reads game knowledge base)
├── PlannerAgent    (builds turn strategy)
└── ExecutorAgent   (applies actions to game state)

docs.title: 3.2 Parallel Fan-Out
docs.text:
When subagents are independent, run them concurrently. Merge results after all complete.

Orchestrator ──┬── AgentA (concurrent)
               ├── AgentB (concurrent)
               └── AgentC (concurrent)
               └── [merge results]

docs.title: 3.3 Human-in-the-Loop
docs.text: For irreversible or high-stakes actions, pause and request human confirmation before proceeding. Design the pause point explicitly in the workflow.

docs.title: 4.1 Context Window Discipline
docs.text:
• Keep system prompts concise and focused — every token costs reasoning capacity
  • Pass only relevant context to subagents, not the entire parent context
  • Summarize long histories before passing them downstream

docs.title: 4.2 Memory Tiers
docs.text: Tier Mechanism Use Case In-context Messages in current window Short-term reasoning Scratchpad Tool writes to temp file/store Inter-step state Persistent Database / vector store Long-term knowledge External Git, files, APIs Source of truth

docs.title: 4.3 State Passing
docs.text: Prefer explicit state passing over implicit shared globals. Each agent invocation should receive everything it needs as input and return everything relevant as output.

docs.title: 5.1 JSON-Serializable I/O
docs.text: All tool inputs and outputs must be JSON-serializable. Avoid returning objects that cannot be represented in a tool result.

docs.title: 5.2 Idempotent Tools Where Possible
docs.text: Design tools to be safe to call multiple times with the same arguments. This makes retry logic safe.

docs.title: 5.3 Typed Schemas
docs.text:
Always declare full input schemas (using Pydantic or JSON Schema). This prevents hallucinated parameters and enables static validation.

class MovePlayerInput(BaseModel):
    player_id: str
    direction: Literal["north", "south", "east", "west"]
    steps: int = Field(ge=1, le=100)

docs.title: 5.4 Fail Loudly
docs.text: Return structured error objects rather than silently failing or returning empty results. Include an error field and a message explaining what went wrong.

docs.title: 6.1 System Prompt Structure
docs.text: A good agent system prompt includes: 1. Role — who the agent is and what it's responsible for 2. Context — relevant background (game state, rules, constraints) 3. Goal — what success looks like 4. Tools — brief description of available tools (the model sees schemas, but a one-line summary in the prompt helps) 5. Constraints — what the agent must not do

docs.title: 6.2 Avoid Ambiguity
docs.text:
Vague instructions produce inconsistent behavior. Replace:

"Handle the player's turn"

With:

"Given the current game state, choose exactly one action from the available_actions list. Call the execute_action tool with your chosen action. If no valid actions exist, call report_stalemate."

docs.title: 6.3 Chain-of-Thought for Complex Reasoning
docs.text: For tasks requiring multi-step reasoning (planning, strategy), instruct the agent to reason before acting. Extended thinking or explicit scratchpad use improves decision quality.

docs.title: 7.1 Unit Test Tools Independently
docs.text: Every agent tool must have unit tests that run outside of any agent loop. Test both happy paths and edge cases.

docs.title: 7.2 Trace Agent Runs
docs.text: Log the full sequence of tool calls, inputs, and outputs for every agent run. This is essential for debugging unexpected behavior.

docs.title: 7.3 Regression Tests via Fixtures
docs.text: Record representative agent runs as fixtures. Run them as regression tests to catch model behavior changes across updates.

docs.title: 7.4 Evals Over Examples
docs.text: Use structured evaluation sets (input → expected outcome) rather than manual spot-checking. Automate scoring where possible.

docs.title: 8.1 Output Validation
docs.text: Validate agent outputs before acting on them. Never pass raw model output directly into system calls, databases, or other agents without sanitization.

docs.title: 8.2 Action Reversibility
docs.text: Prefer reversible actions. Before implementing irreversible operations, require explicit confirmation (human or higher-trust agent).

docs.title: 8.3 Scope Limiting
docs.text: Define explicit allowed/denied action sets per agent. Reject any action that falls outside the defined scope, even if the model requests it.

docs.title: 8.4 Rate Limiting and Cost Controls
docs.text: Set maximum token budgets and tool call limits per agent run. Unbounded loops are a real risk in agentic systems.

docs.title: 9.1 Rebuild Structure
docs.text: Each game rebuild lives in rebuilds/<game-name>/ as a git submodule. The rebuild must include its own AGENTS.md extending this base document with game-specific agent context.

docs.title: 9.2 Shared Tooling
docs.text: Common tools shared across rebuilds (e.g., logging, state serialization) are maintained in this meta-repository and imported by each rebuild.

docs.title: 9.3 MkDocs Documentation
docs.text: All design decisions, agent architectures, and lessons learned are documented in docs/ and published via GitHub Pages (MkDocs Material theme).

docs.title: 10. References and Further Reading
docs.text:
• Anthropic Agent SDK documentation
  • Building Effective Agents — Anthropic
  • Claude Code AGENTS.md convention
  • LangGraph documentation
  • Evaluating LLM Agents

docs.title: Rebuilds
docs.text: Each rebuild is an independent repository linked as a git submodule under rebuilds/.

docs.title: Adding a Rebuild
docs.text:
To add a new rebuild:

git submodule add https://github.com/MarcoAAlmeida/<game-name> rebuilds/<game-name>
git commit -m "Add <game-name> as submodule"

Each rebuild is expected to have its own AGENTS.md extending the base knowledge base with game-specific agent context.

docs.title: Current Rebuilds
docs.text: Game Repository Status Karriers MarcoAAlmeida/karriers In Progress

docs.title: Karriers
docs.text:
Repository: MarcoAAlmeida/karriers

Submodule path: rebuilds/karriers/

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Karriers is a carrier-based strategy game rebuild using agentic frameworks. The goal is to model game logic, turn management, and AI opponents as a multi-agent system.

docs.title: Agent Architecture
docs.text:
This section will be expanded as the rebuild progresses.

Refer to the rebuild's own AGENTS.md at rebuilds/karriers/AGENTS.md for game-specific agent patterns.
